dataCollectionProcess: We construct our dataset from 25M openly available samples from codeforces.com for a total of 7514 distinct problems. Each sample represents a potential solution to a problem, and a problem can be solved by employing a set of algorithmic techniques referred to as problem tags.
dataPreprocessing: A novel data splitting and data selection schema is proposed for balancing data distributions over multiple attributes based on geometric mean and graph-theoretic principle. The data selection is formulated as a circulation problem with lower and upper bounds within a flow network, controlling minimum and maximum samples per problem and per tag. Validation and test sets are created from a held-out set of 1354 problems (Nh=1354) to prevent data leakage, ensuring problems in validation and test sets are not seen in training.
datasetAvailability: Publicly available. The dataset can be accessed via the Hugging Face load_dataset() API (datasets.load_dataset("NTU-NLP-sg/xCodeEval", ...)) or downloaded as a git LFS repository from Hugging Face. License: cc-by-4.0.
datasetNoise: Not found
datasetSize: 25M document-level coding examples (16.5B tokens) from about 7.5K unique problems. Size category on HuggingFace: 1M<n<10M. Per-task split counts are detailed in Table 2 of the paper (e.g., Code Compilation train: 19,915,150 samples; Tag Classification train: 5,494,008 samples; Program Synthesis train: 5,538,841 samples).
datasetType: Multilingual multitask benchmark consisting of code and text (natural language) data. Languages: code, English. Tasks include: Translation, Token Classification, Text Retrieval, text2text-generation, and more. Data format is JSONL files stored via Git LFS.
intendedUse: A benchmark for evaluating large language models on 7 tasks involving code understanding, generation, translation, and retrieval: Tag Classification, Code Compilation, Program Synthesis, Code Translation, Automatic Program Repair (APR), Code-Code Retrieval, and NL-Code Retrieval. It employs execution-based evaluation using the ExecEval engine.
knownBias: Not found

dataCollectionProcess: MinerU-HTML is applied to extract content from Common Crawl snapshots (CC-2023-06 and CC-2023-14), converting raw HTML WARC archives into Markdown format. The released version of AICC is based on more recent snapshots (CC-2025-08 and CC-2025-13) to provide the community with the most up-to-date data.
dataPreprocessing: MinerU-HTML is a novel two-stage extraction pipeline that reformulates content extraction as a sequence labeling problem solved by a compact 0.6B-parameter language model. It explicitly categorizes semantic elements before converting to Markdown, preserving document structure including formulas, code blocks, and tables. AICC and its Trafilatura-extracted counterpart (TfCC) undergo identical filtering and deduplication.
datasetAvailability: Publicly available on Hugging Face (opendatalab/AICC). License: CC BY 4.0, requiring attribution when used.
datasetNoise: It is derived from Common Crawl web pages and may contain biased or sensitive content; users are responsible for ethical and lawful usage in research or applications.
datasetSize: 7.3-trillion token multilingual corpus. On HuggingFace: 4.84B rows (train split), size category 1Bâ10B.
datasetType: Text generation dataset, multilingual, stored in Parquet format. Data is web content extracted from Common Crawl HTML pages and converted to Markdown. Tags: common-crawl, html-parsing, web-corpus, markdown.
hasSensitivePersonalInformation: The dataset is derived from Common Crawl web pages and may contain biased or sensitive content.
intendedUse: A large-scale pretraining corpus optimized for language model training. Demonstrates that high-quality HTML extraction can rival or exceed the benefits of aggressive filtering strategies for downstream model performance. Also suitable for Deep Research Agents, RAG applications, and training data generation.
knownBias: It is derived from Common Crawl web pages and may contain biased or sensitive content; users are responsible for ethical and lawful usage.

dataCollectionProcess: The dataset was created with an internal crowdsourcing effort at Google. The Mostly Basic Programming Problems dataset contains 974 short Python programs constructed by crowd-sourcing to an internal pool of crowdworkers who have basic knowledge of Python. Crowdsourcing participants were asked to write a short problem statement, a single self-contained Python function solving the problem specified, and three test cases that check for semantic correctness of the function. Participants also provided a ground-truth solution that passes all three test cases. Participants were instructed to write descriptions concrete enough that a human would be able to translate them into code without clarifications. They were also instructed to write code that is self-contained and that does not print any results to the console. Use of internet references was allowed.
dataPreprocessing: The full dataset was created first and a subset then underwent a second round to improve the task descriptions. The authors manually inspected, edited, and pruned a subset of the questions, yielding 426 hand-verified questions (referred to as the edited dataset). For each question in the edited dataset, they ensured it had a standard Python function signature, that it was unambiguous to a human, and that its test cases accurately reflected the text description. All reference code was programmatically checked to pass all tests under Python 3.6. The sanitized split aims at addressing task description quality issues by having a second round of annotators improve the dataset.
datasetAvailability: Publicly available. The dataset is open-sourced on GitHub (https://github.com/google-research/google-research/tree/master/mbpp) and accessible via HuggingFace load_dataset() API (load_dataset("google-research-datasets/mbpp")). License: cc-by-4.0.
datasetNoise: Some questions used uncommon function signatures (such as passing in a list and its length as two separate arguments to a function), lacked detail, were somewhat ambiguous (e.g., "Write a python function to count the number of squares in a rectangle."), or performed unexpected operations in a function that were paired with the provided tests (e.g., casting a float to an int before returning it, with the test performing integer comparisons). Since the task descriptions might not be expressive enough to solve the task.
datasetSize: 974 programming tasks (short Python functions). Full split: train=374, test=500, validation=90, prompt=10 (total 974 rows); dataset_size=467,938 bytes. Sanitized split: 427 rows. Size category: n<1K.
datasetType: Text dataset (English, monolingual), stored in Parquet format. Contains short Python programming problems with task descriptions, code solutions, and 3 automated test cases per problem. Task category: text2text-generation / code-generation. Annotations created by crowdsourced and expert-generated contributors.
intendedUse: This dataset is used to evaluate code generations. It is a benchmark designed to measure the ability of large language models to synthesize short Python programs from natural language descriptions. Problems are designed to be solvable by entry-level programmers, covering programming fundamentals, standard library functionality. Make sure you execute generated Python code in a safe environment when evaluating against this dataset as generated code could be harmful.
knownBias: Of a random sample of 100 questions: 58% were mathematical in nature, 43% involve list processing, 19% require string processing, 9% involve integer sequences, and 2% center around the use of other data structures. Since the task descriptions might not be expressive enough to solve the task.

dataCollectionProcess: The dataset includes problems, solutions and test cases scraped from the Codeforces platform, along with existing public competitive programming datasets. The training dataset combines newly scraped data from Codeforces (Mirzayanov, 2020) with existing data from Description2Code (Caballero et al., 2016) and CodeNet (Puri et al., 2021). The validation and test splits consist entirely of newly scraped Codeforces problems. Sources by site: Aizu (https://judge.u-aizu.ac.jp) from CodeNet; AtCoder (https://atcoder.jp) from CodeNet; CodeChef (https://www.codechef.com) from description2code; Codeforces (https://codeforces.com) from description2code and Codeforces; HackerEarth (https://www.hackerearth.com) from description2code. Problems include test cases in the form of paired inputs and outputs, as well as both correct and incorrect human solutions in a variety of languages.
dataPreprocessing: A strict temporal split was adopted to guard against data leakage: all training data in CodeContests was publicly released on or before 2021/07/14; validation problems appeared between 2021/07/15 and 2021/09/20; test problems were published after 2021/09/21. Data was cleaned to improve quality and consistency and to avoid duplication issues involved in merging datasets (procedure outlined in Appendix B.2 of the paper). Additional generated tests were added to each problem to reduce false positive rates: CodeContests achieves an average of 203.7 tests per problem, reducing the false positive rate from 30-60% in existing datasets to just 4%. Problems with insufficient tests were filtered out.
datasetAvailability: Publicly available. The dataset can be downloaded via Google Cloud Storage: "gsutil -m cp -r gs://dm-code_contests /tmp" (~3 GiB). Also accessible on HuggingFace (deepmind/code_contests). License for non-code materials: CC BY 4.0. Code licensed under Apache 2.0. The GitHub repository (https://github.com/google-deepmind/code_contests) has been archived (read-only as of December 6, 2024).
datasetNoise: The incorrect and correct solutions attached to problems are not guaranteed to compile and execute in the exact same way as in their original contest website (for example different compiler versions or flags or different library versions). Some of the solutions will fail compilation, or will produce sandbox violations, especially if they are incorrect. Prior to adding generated tests, the raw CodeContests dataset had a false positive rate of 62% and a false positive or slow rate of 88%.
datasetSize: Train: 13,328 problems (3,760 rows on HuggingFace); Validation: 117 problems; Test: 165 problems. Total ~4,040 rows on HuggingFace. Per-problem averages in train split: 2.0 example tests, 14.8 hidden tests, 79.1 generated tests, 493.4 C++ solutions, 281.1 Python solutions, 147.9 Java solutions. Full dataset download size approximately 3 GiB.
datasetType: Competitive programming dataset containing problem descriptions (natural language, English), public/private/generated test cases (paired inputs and outputs), correct and incorrect human solutions in C++, Python, and Java, plus problem metadata (difficulty ratings, tags, cf_contest_id, cf_points, cf_rating, time_limit, memory_limit_bytes, source). Modalities: Tabular and Text. Format on HuggingFace: Parquet. Task category: Translation. Data stored as ContestProblem protocol buffers in Riegeli format on Cloud Storage.
intendedUse: CodeContests is a competitive programming dataset for machine learning. This dataset was used when training AlphaCode. It is intended for fine-tuning and evaluation of code generation models on competitive programming problems. The dataset provides both a training set and an evaluation benchmark where solutions are evaluated by executing them on test cases and comparing output with the expected correct output, mirroring the structure of real programming competitions.
knownBias: The dataset is sourced predominantly from Codeforces for validation and test splits, and combines Codeforces with Description2Code and CodeNet for training. Human solutions are available only for the most popular submission languages: C++, Python, and Java. The dataset includes a field "is_description_translated" indicating whether a problem description was translated, and an "untranslated_description" field. Neither difficulty ratings nor tags are visible at competition time.

dataCollectionProcess: FineFineWeb is constructed by applying a coarse-fine domain-specific recall pipeline on top of FineWeb (which is itself derived from Common Crawl). For each Domain of Interest (DoI), the collection process follows these steps: (1) Seed Data Collection: A human-curated collection of URLs is used as positive seed examples for each domain. (2) A binary FastText model is trained per domain using the labeled seed data. (3) The FastText model performs coarse recall on FineWeb, generating Coarse DoI Data. (4) The Coarse DoI Data is labeled using Qwen2-72B-Instruct to produce 100K DoI Positive Data and 50K DoI Negative Data, with the latter further augmented with 50K negative samples from earlier FastText training. (5) A BERT model is trained using this labeled data. (6) The BERT model performs fine recall on the Coarse DoI Data, producing the refined DoI subset of FineFineWeb.
dataPreprocessing: Coarse-Fine Recall Iteration: The workflow of coarse and fine recall iterates for 3 rounds with the following adjustments: (a) FastText is re-trained using updated seed data, which combines BERT-recalled samples, BERT-dropped samples, and previously labeled seed data. (b) The BERT model keeps frozen during subsequent iterations. (c) Steps for training FastText, coarse recall, and fine recall are repeated without re-labeling data with Qwen2-Instruct models. Proportional weighted sampling of the domain subsets is performed based on the sample size of each domain, with a total of 1 billion tokens sampled from the domain subsets. The BGE-M3 model is used to compute the embeddings of the samples in each domain subset (domain embeddings) and embeddings of samples in each benchmark (benchmark embeddings). MMD distance and Wasserstein distance are calculated between domain embeddings and benchmark embeddings.
datasetAvailability: Publicly available on HuggingFace at m-a-p/FineFineWeb. Auto-converted to Parquet format via HuggingFace Data Studio. License: Apache-2.0.
datasetNoise: Not found
datasetSize: Default subset (train split): approximately 4.89 billion rows. Size category: 1B to 10B rows. Columns include: url, date, file_path, language_score, token_count, dump, global_id, lang, text, domain, round. The domain column contains 67 distinct domain values.
datasetType: Tasks: Text Classification, Text Generation. Modalities: Tabular, Text. Language: English (monolingual). Format: Parquet (auto-converted from JSON). The dataset contains web-crawled text documents from FineWeb (Common Crawl), organized into 67 fine-grained domain subsets. Each record includes the source URL, crawl date, Common Crawl file path, language score, token count, CC dump name, a global document ID, language tag, text content, domain label, and round number.
intendedUse: FineFineWeb is intended as a fine-grained domain web corpus for LLM pretraining research. It is described in the citation as "A Comprehensive Study on Fine-grained Domain Web Corpus." The dataset supports domain-specific analysis and downstream LLM training by providing domain-labeled subsets of FineWeb. It is also used to analyze the correlation between domain-specific data and benchmark performance via MMD and Wasserstein distances.
knownBias: The two code-related benchmarks, MBPP and HumanEval, exhibit relatively large distances from nearly all domains, indicating that the proportion of code data in the training set is relatively small. For TriviaQA, which emphasizes factual knowledge over reasoning, domains rich in world knowledge such as literature, history, and library science demonstrate higher correlation rankings.

dataCollectionProcess: Articles were selected only if they fit the Good or Featured article criteria specified by editors on Wikipedia. These articles have been reviewed by humans and are considered well written, factually accurate, broad in coverage, neutral in point of view, and stable. This resulted in 23,805 Good articles and 4,790 Featured articles. The text for each article was extracted using the Wikipedia API. Extracting the raw text from Wikipedia mark-up is nontrivial due to the large number of macros in use, which include metric conversion, abbreviations, language notation, and date handling.
dataPreprocessing: Once extracted, specific sections which primarily featured lists were removed by default. Other minor bugs, such as sort keys and Edit buttons that leaked in from the HTML, were also removed. Mathematical formulae and LaTeX code were replaced with <formula> tokens. Normalization and tokenization were performed using the Moses tokenizer, slightly augmented to further split numbers (e.g., 8,600 â 8 @,@ 600) and with some additional minor fixes. A vocabulary was constructed by discarding all words with a count below 3. Words outside of the vocabulary were mapped to the <unk> token, also a part of the vocabulary. For the non-raw variants (wikitext-103-v1 and wikitext-2-v1), out-of-vocabulary tokens are replaced with the <unk> token. The raw variants (wikitext-103-raw-v1 and wikitext-2-raw-v1) contain the raw tokens before the addition of the <unk> tokens. The dataset is provided in the same format and following the same conventions as that of the Penn Treebank dataset.
datasetAvailability: Publicly available for download at the WikiText dataset site (homepage: https://blog.einstein.ai/the-wikitext-long-term-dependency-language-modeling-dataset/). Also hosted on Hugging Face at Salesforce/wikitext (auto-converted to Parquet format). The dataset is available under the Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike License (CC BY-SA 4.0). Licenses listed on HuggingFace: cc-by-sa-3.0 and gfdl.
datasetNoise: Not found
datasetSize:
The full WikiText dataset is over 103 million words in size. It is available in two sizes:
WikiText-2: 2,088,628 training tokens, 217,646 validation tokens, 245,569 test tokens; 600 training articles, 60 validation articles, 60 test articles; vocabulary size 33,278; OoV rate 2.6%.
WikiText-103: 103,227,021 training tokens, 217,646 validation tokens, 245,569 test tokens; 28,475 training articles, 60 validation articles, 60 test articles; vocabulary size 267,735; OoV rate 0.4%.
HuggingFace size category: 1Mâ10M rows. Total downloaded dataset files: 391.41 MB. Size of the generated dataset: 1.12 GB. Total disk used: 1.52 GB.
Subset row counts (train / validation / test):
wikitext-103-raw-v1: 1,801,350 / 3,760 / 4,358
wikitext-103-v1: 1,801,350 / 3,760 / 4,358
wikitext-2-raw-v1: 36,718 / 3,760 / 4,358
wikitext-2-v1: 36,718 / 3,760 / 4,358
datasetType: A collection of over 100 million tokens extracted from the set of verified Good and Featured articles on Wikipedia. Tasks: Text Generation, Fill-Mask. Sub-tasks: language-modeling, masked-language-modeling. Modalities: Text. Language: English (monolingual). Format: Parquet (auto-converted on HuggingFace). Each subset comes in two variants: Raw (for character-level work) containing raw tokens before <unk> substitution, and Non-raw (for word-level work) containing only in-vocabulary tokens with out-of-vocabulary tokens replaced by <unk>. The dataset features original case, punctuation, and numbers (unlike the Penn Treebank). Single data field per record: text (string).
intendedUse: Introduced as a new benchmark dataset for language modeling to evaluate how well language models can exploit longer contexts and deal with more realistic vocabularies and larger corpora. The authors state: "we also introduce the freely available WikiText corpus." The dataset is intended as a platform to improve handling of rare words and the usage of long term dependencies in language modeling. It supports word-level and character-level language modeling experiments. Supported tasks: Text Generation, Fill-Mask, language-modeling, masked-language-modeling.
knownBias: Compared to the Penn Treebank, WikiText-2 is over 2 times larger and WikiText-103 is over 110 times larger. Other larger-scale language modeling datasets either have restrictive licensing which prevents widespread use or have randomized sentence ordering (as in the One Billion Word Benchmark), which is unrealistic for most language use and prevents effective learning and evaluation of longer-term dependencies. No explicit bias discussion is provided beyond this dataset comparison context.

dataCollectionProcess: The pretraining dataset was curated by the authors to be aligned with GIFT-Eval (the associated benchmark). It contains 71 univariate and 17 multivariate datasets, spanning seven domains and 13 frequencies. The dataset was specifically designed to have no leakage issue with the GIFT-Eval train/test split, so it can be used to pretrain foundation models that can be fairly evaluated on GIFT-Eval. The paper notes that the pretraining component features 88 datasets including 240 billion data points (the paper also states 230 billion data points in the dataset section and abstract). The authors note that the pre-training datasets of TimesFM, Chronos, and Moirai exhibit partial data leakage issues for GIFT-Eval, which this pretraining dataset was designed to avoid. Data sources for individual constituent datasets are listed in Appendix E of the paper.
dataPreprocessing: For the train/test portion of GIFT-Eval, data is stored in the Arrow format (Richardson et al., 2023), ensuring efficient integration into deep learning pipelines. A non-overlapping rolling evaluation method is employed for the test split. No explicit additional preprocessing steps are described specifically for the GiftEvalPretrain dataset beyond the collection and curation described in Appendix E of the paper.
datasetAvailability: Publicly available on HuggingFace at Salesforce/GiftEvalPretrain. License: Apache-2.0 (as listed on HuggingFace and in the GitHub repository). Code and leaderboard available at https://github.com/SalesforceAIResearch/gift-eval. The GitHub repository states: "This repository is intended for research purposes only." The HuggingFace page states: "This release is for research purposes only in support of an academic paper."
datasetNoise: Not found
datasetSize: The pretraining dataset contains 71 univariate and 17 multivariate datasets, spanning seven domains and 13 frequencies, totaling 4.5 million time series and 230 billion data points (the paper's introduction also refers to 240 billion data points and 88 datasets in one passage). HuggingFace size category: 1M<n<10M rows. Dataset preview columns: item_id (string), start (timestamp), freq (string), target (sequence), past_feat_dynamic_real (sequence).
datasetType: Time series pretraining dataset. Task: Time Series Forecasting. Modality: Time-series. Tags: timeseries, forecasting, benchmark, gifteval. Contains 71 univariate and 17 multivariate datasets across seven domains and 13 frequencies. The domains covered include: Econ/Fin, Energy, Healthcare, Nature, Sales, Transport, and Web/CloudOps (as described for the associated GIFT-Eval benchmark). Each record includes item_id, start timestamp, frequency, target values (sequence), and past_feat_dynamic_real (past dynamic real-valued features, sequence).
intendedUse: Intended as a non-leaking pretraining dataset to facilitate the effective pretraining and evaluation of time series foundation models that can be fairly evaluated on GIFT-Eval. From HuggingFace: "Pretraining dataset aligned with GIFT-Eval... can be used to pretrain foundation models that can be fairly evaluated on GIFT-Eval." From HuggingFace Ethical Considerations: "This release is for research purposes only in support of an academic paper." From GitHub: "This repository is intended for research purposes only." The authors pretrain Moirai models using this pretraining split to demonstrate its utility.
knownBias: The dataset is explicitly designed to have no leakage issue with the GIFT-Eval train/test split. The paper notes that the pre-training datasets of TimesFM, Chronos, and Moirai exhibit partial data leakage issues for GIFT-Eval â a problem that GiftEvalPretrain was curated to avoid. No other bias or limitation is explicitly discussed for this pretraining dataset specifically.

dataCollectionProcess: The Code Clippy GitHub dataset was created from the public GitHub dataset on Google BigQuery. For the broader Code Clippy dataset (which feeds into this GitHub subset), repositories were collected from two sources: (1) https://seart-ghs.si.usi.ch/ (SEART GitHub Search) using the following criteria: more than 10 GitHub stars, more than 2 commits, must have a licence, exclude forks, and size less than 70708 bytes; and (2) the GitHub portion of The Pile (performed on July 7th, 2021). The helper script from https://github.com/EleutherAI/github-downloader was used to download the repositories. Files were scraped from the downloaded repositories, but files with certain extensions associated with binary or other non-textual/autogenerated content were ignored, and the output was converted into the LM_Dataformat format.
dataPreprocessing: The repositories are filtered for duplicate files. Filtering is performed by regexing each file in each repository to obtain a list of "variables" (the tokens which only contain alphanumeric characters) and then filtering out any files which contain the same sequence of "variables". The deduplication script is available at https://github.com/ncoop57/gpt-code-clippy/tree/camera-ready/data_processing/deduplication. There is a version containing duplicates (209 GBs compressed) and one where exact duplicates (132 GBs compressed) are removed. A known bug was discovered: file names in the dataset are obsolete/misleading. Since filtering for the training dataset is done using the file extension, wrong datapoints may have been included and correct datapoints may have been missed. The CodedotAI team noted they are working on adding additional filters and cleaning the dataset.
datasetAvailability: Publicly available on HuggingFace at CodedotAI/code_clippy_github. License listed on HuggingFace: MIT. GitHub repository license: Apache-2.0. The final deduplicated dataset is also available at https://the-eye.eu/public/AI/training_data/code_clippy_data/code_clippy_dedup_data/. The dataset without duplicates filtered out is also available at the same URL. Streaming is recommended due to dataset size: from datasets import load_dataset; ds = load_dataset("CodedotAI/code_clippy_github", streaming=True).
datasetNoise: No filtering was performed on licensed code, meaning the dataset may contain restrictively licensed code. A known bug was discovered during scraping: file names in the dataset are obsolete/misleading. Since filtering for the training dataset is done using the file extension, wrong datapoints may have been included and correct datapoints from languages of choice may have been missed. Since this data was collected from public repositories, there exists potential for personal and sensitive information to be included in the data through developers accidentally or on purpose uploading their secret keys, passwords, API keys, emails, etc.
datasetSize: The Code Clippy dataset consists of various public codebases from GitHub in 22 programming languages with 23 extensions, totaling about 16 TB of data when uncompressed. HuggingFace size category: 1M to 10M rows. The dataset with duplicates is 209 GBs compressed; the deduplicated version is 132 GBs compressed. The dataset is organized into 384 subsets by programming language and license combination. Only a train split is provided.
datasetType: A collection of public codebases from GitHub in 22 programming languages with 23 extensions. Modality: Text. Sub-task: language-modeling. Language: code (multilingual/multi-programming-language). Task category: text-generation. The dataset is organized into 384 subsets by programming language and license. Programming languages include: C, C++, C#, CSS, Dart, GO, HTML, Java, JavaScript, Jupyter Notebooks (Python), Kotlin, Lisp, Matlab, PHP, Perl, Python, R, Ruby, Rust, SQL, Shell, Swift, TypeScript. Each record contains: code_text (string), repo_name (string), file_path (string), language (string), license (string), size (int32). Licenses represented include: apache-2.0, agpl-3.0, artistic-2.0, bsd-2-clause, bsd-3-clause, cc0-1.0, epl-1.0, gpl-2.0, gpl-3.0, isc, lgpl-2.1, lgpl-3.0, mit, mpl-2.0, unlicense.
hasSensitivePersonalInformation: Yes â potential exists. Since this data was collected from public repositories, there exists potential for personal and sensitive information to be included in the data through developers accidentally or on purpose uploading their secret keys, passwords, API keys, emails, etc.
intendedUse: The goal of this dataset is to provide a training set for pretraining large language models on code data, for helping software engineering researchers better understand their impacts on software-related tasks such as autocompletion of code. GPT-Code-Clippy (GPT-CC) is described as an open source version of GitHub Copilot, a language model fine-tuned on publicly available code from GitHub. The models are evaluated on the APPS and HumanEval datasets. A Visual Studio Code extension using the HuggingFace Inference API is available. The annotators/source language producers are identified as software developers; no annotation was performed.
knownBias: The programming languages most represented in this dataset are JavaScript and Python. Therefore, other still-popular languages such as C and C++ are less represented, and model performance for these languages will be comparatively less. No filtering was performed on licensed code, meaning the dataset may contain restrictively licensed code. The referenced arXiv paper (Codex paper, OpenAI) notes that code generation models can generate code with structure that reflects stereotypes about gender, race, emotion, class, the structure of names, and other characteristics. The paper also notes that Codex models trained on code can generate racist, denigratory, and otherwise harmful outputs as code comments. Legal implications: training AI systems on Internet data such as public GitHub repositories has been identified as an instance of "fair use," but there has been little to no previous case law on such usage of licensed publicly available code, and it is unclear the legal ramifications of using a language model trained on this dataset.

dataCollectionProcess: Objects in the dataset are sourced from Sketchfab, a leading online platform for managing, viewing, and distributing 3D models. Objects selected for OBJAVERSE have a distributable Creative Commons license and were obtained using Sketchfab's public API. Aside from licensing consideration, models marked as restricted due to objectionable or adult thematic content were excluded from the dataset. In total, OBJAVERSE contains over 800K 3D assets designed by over 100K artists.
dataPreprocessing: Models marked as restricted due to objectionable or adult thematic content were excluded from the dataset. For the OBJAVERSE-LVIS subset, 500 candidate objects per category were selected using a combination of predictions from a CLIP classifier to assign objects to one of 1156 LVIS categories, yielding a 47K LVIS categorized object subset called OBJAVERSE-LVIS.
datasetAvailability: Publicly available. The dataset as a whole is licensed under the ODC-By v1.0 license. Individual objects are licensed under Creative Commons licenses (CC-BY 4.0: 721K objects, CC-BY-NC 4.0: 25K objects, CC-BY-NC-SA 4.0: 52K objects, CC-BY-SA 4.0: 16K objects, CC0 1.0: 3.5K objects). Available on Hugging Face at allenai/objaverse.
datasetNoise: Not found
datasetSize: Over 800K 3D assets (818K objects) spanning 21K categories, designed by over 100K artists. OBJAVERSE-LVIS is a subset of 47K objects across 1156 LVIS categories.
datasetType: 3D object dataset. Objects include descriptive captions, tags, and animations. Metadata includes a name, assignments to a set of fixed categories, a set of unrestricted tags, and a natural language description. The dataset also contains many scanned assets in addition to artist-designed objects.
intendedUse: The dataset is intended for use in: training generative 3D models, improving tail category segmentation on the LVIS benchmark (via Copy+Paste augmentation), training open-vocabulary object-navigation models for Embodied AI (e.g., populating procedurally generated simulated environments in ProcTHOR), and creating benchmarks for robustness analysis of vision models (e.g., evaluating CLIP-style backbones under perspective shifts).
knownBias: Not found

dataCollectionProcess: The dataset unifies data from multiple real-world and synthetic sources: (1) nuScenes and Waymo â both provide camera images, LiDAR sweeps, and object-level annotations from real-world driving; (2) CARLA â a simulation engine used to generate virtual driving scenes from which "perfect" 3D occupancy labels (meshes, object states, etc.) can be extracted; (3) OpenCOOD â built on CARLA, it offers multi-vehicle cooperation scenarios, and its data-generation scripts were extended to export 3D occupancy from simulated meshes.
dataPreprocessing: Since nuScenes and Waymo do not directly include 3D occupancy labels, occupancy ground truth is synthesized via three pseudo-labeling pipelines: Occ3D, OpenOccupancy, and SurroundOcc. This multi-tool approach increases robustness and diversity in labeled outputs. Per-voxel forward and backward flow annotations are computed separately for dynamic foreground objects (e.g., Car, Pedestrian) and static background environment (e.g., Road, Vegetation), then merged into a unified flow field. For 2D tasks, the height dimension is collapsed via a priority scheme (e.g., Pedestrian > Car > Road), such that each vertical pillar adopts the label of its highest-priority voxel.
datasetAvailability: Publicly available. The dataset is hosted on Hugging Face (tasl-lab/uniocc) and can be downloaded via the Hugging Face CLI. Alternative downloads are also available via Google Drive and Baidu Pan. License: MIT.
datasetNoise: Widely used driving datasets (nuScenes and Waymo) lack official occupancy annotations; existing research relies on pseudo ground truth labels derived from LiDAR point cloud heuristics or manual labeling. These pseudo labels often capture only the reflective surfaces (e.g., car sides hit by LiDAR), failing to represent the true 3D occupancy of the scene. Models trained on these suboptimal labels inevitably produce suboptimal results. Standard metrics like IoU cannot reveal such quality issues because they compare predictions solely against the flawed pseudo labels.
datasetSize: Total: 14.2 hours of driving data, 2152 scenarios, at 2 Hz and/or 10 Hz sampling rates across all sources. Individual subset sizes (from HuggingFace): NuScenes-via-Occ3D-2Hz-train: 700 scenes, 28,130 instances, 41.2 GB; NuScenes-via-OpenOccupancy-2Hz-train: 28.3 GB; NuScenes-via-SurroundOcc-2Hz-train: 28.1 GB; Waymo-via-Occ3D-2Hz-train: 798 scenes, 31,880 instances, 59.5 GB; Waymo-via-Occ3D-10Hz-train: 798 scenes, 158,081 instances, 286.6 GB; Carla-2Hz-train: 11 scenes, 8,400 instances, 9.3 GB; Carla-10Hz-train: 11 scenes, 42,200 instances, 46.5 GB; OPV2V-10Hz-train: 43 scenes, 18,676 instances, 49.8 GB.
datasetType: 3D occupancy dataset for autonomous driving. Each timestep (.npz file) contains: a 3D semantic occupancy grid (L x W x H) with semantic labels; a binary camera FOV mask (L x W x H); forward and backward voxel-level flow fields (L x W x H x 3); ego-to-world transformation matrix (4x4); camera objects with intrinsic (3x3) and extrinsic (4x4) parameters and raw RGB images; object-level annotations with bounding boxes and class labels. Modalities: 3D. Tasks: Image-to-3D, occupancy forecasting, occupancy prediction, flow estimation.
intendedUse: UniOcc is intended for: (1) Occupancy Forecasting â predicting future 3D occupancy grids over time given historical occupancies or camera inputs; (2) Occupancy Prediction â generating detailed 3D occupancy grids from camera inputs; (3) Flow Estimation â providing forward and backward voxel-level flow fields for motion modeling and object tracking; (4) Multi-Domain Dataset Integration â supporting major autonomous driving datasets (nuScenes, Waymo, CARLA, etc.) with consistent annotation and evaluation pipelines; (5) Cooperative Autonomous Driving â enabling multi-agent occupancy fusion and forecasting.
knownBias: Not found
sensorUsed: nuScenes and Waymo source data was collected with cameras (multi-view RGB) and LiDAR sensors. Camera intrinsics (3x3 matrix) and extrinsics (4x4 transformation matrix from camera to ego vehicle LiDAR) are stored per frame. CARLA and OpenCOOD data is synthetically generated via simulation.

dataCollectionProcess: The dataset is a compilation of multiple publicly available source datasets, all sourced from Hugging Face. The mixture was selected to maximize the potential for emergent reasoning behaviors rather than for optimal benchmark performance, and is heavily skewed towards code and mathematical reasoning data. Instruction data is directly mixed into the pretraining data following Allen-Zhu and Li (2024). The data distribution across categories is as follows: generic-text (28.71%), code (25.36%), scientific-text (18.73%), synthetic-text (8.14%), longform-text (7.50%), math (6.14%), generic-instruct (2.09%), Q&A-text (1.58%), math-instruct (1.51%), writing-instruct (0.12%), misc-reasoning (0.11%). Due to compute and time constraints, the mixture was not ablated. A machine-readable list of all source datasets is provided in sources.yaml in the repository.
dataPreprocessing: A vocabulary of 65,536 tokens was constructed via BPE (Byte-Pair Encoding), with the tokenizer trained directly on the instruction data split of the pretraining corpus to maximize tokenization efficiency on the target domain. The pre-tokenization regex was substantially modified to better support code, contractions, and LaTeX. A <|begin_text|> token is included at the start of every document. Tokenized documents are packed into sequences of length 4,096. Document ends that would otherwise lack previous context are discarded (to fix the "grounding problem"), except for several long-document sources of mathematical content, which are preserved in their entirety. Chat templating is applied to the data, which spells out the name of the model (Huginn); this can be replaced by regexing for <|begin_header|>Huginn<|end_header|>, or token IDs [65506, 72, 3125, 20359, 65507]. The dataset is provided as 4,096 parquet files for train and validation each, exactly corresponding to the 4,096 AMD GPUs used for data-parallel training.
datasetAvailability: Publicly available on Hugging Face at tomg-group-umd/huginn-dataset. No blanket license can be provided for the aggregated dataset; each subcomponent operates under a separate license, all listed in the dataset card. License is listed as "other" on Hugging Face. Scripts for downloading and processing the data are also publicly available at github.com/seal-rg/recurrent-pretraining.
datasetNoise: Due to compute and time constraints, the data mixture was not ablated, so some choices that were made may be suboptimal. The prepared data includes chat templating that spells out the name of the model (Huginn), which needs to be replaced if the data is used to train a different model.
datasetSize: 274,349,409 total rows (261M train, 13.7M validation). Each row is 4,097 tokens long. Total: 274,349,409 Ã 4,097 = 1.124 trillion tokens. The Huginn-0125 model was trained on 800 billion tokens. Size category on Hugging Face: 100Mâ1B rows.
datasetType: Text generation dataset, English, stored in Parquet format. Each row contains a text field (human-readable) and an input_ids field (tokenized sequence of 4,097 tokens using a custom 65,536-token BPE tokenizer). Tags: code, math, reasoning, llm. Modalities: Text.
intendedUse: The dataset is the pretraining corpus used to train the Huginn-0125 recurrent-depth language model (3.5 billion parameters, trained for 800 billion tokens). It is intended for pretraining language models with a focus on emergent reasoning, code, and mathematics. It can also be used to train other language models, though note that the prepared data includes chat templating that references the model name "Huginn," which should be removed or replaced when training a different model.
knownBias: The data mixture is heavily skewed towards code and mathematical reasoning data, with generic web text included only in sufficient quantity to allow standard language modeling abilities. The mixture was selected to maximize potential for emergent reasoning behaviors, not necessarily for optimal benchmark performance, and it was not ablated due to compute limitations.

dataCollectionProcess: The base English dataset was assembled by downloading the web extracted text from the April 2019 Common Crawl snapshot. Common Crawl is a publicly-available web archive that provides "web extracted text" by removing markup and other non-text content from scraped HTML files; this process produces around 20TB of scraped text data each month. The multilingual variant (mC4) extends coverage to 108 languages from Common Crawl. The realnewslike variant is a filtered subset retaining only documents from news domains listed in the Open Web Text news URL list.
dataPreprocessing: The following heuristics were applied to Common Crawl web extracted text to create the cleaned "en" variant: (1) Only retained lines that ended in a terminal punctuation mark (period, exclamation mark, question mark, or end quotation mark). (2) Discarded any page with fewer than 3 sentences; only retained lines containing at least 5 words. (3) Removed any page containing any word on the "List of Dirty, Naughty, Obscene or Otherwise Bad Words". (4) Removed any line containing the word "Javascript". (5) Removed any page where the phrase "lorem ipsum" appeared. (6) Removed any page containing a curly bracket "{" (indicative of source code). (7) Removed Wikipedia citation markers (e.g. [1], [citation needed]). (8) Removed any lines containing "terms of use", "privacy policy", "cookie policy", "uses cookies", "use of cookies", or "use cookies". (9) Deduplication: discarded all but one of any three-sentence span occurring more than once in the dataset. (10) Used langdetect to filter out any pages not classified as English with a probability of at least 0.99. The en.noclean variant has none of these filters applied. The en.noblocklist variant is identical to the en variant except that the badwords filter (step 3) was turned off.
datasetAvailability: Publicly available on Hugging Face (allenai/c4) and originally released as part of TensorFlow Datasets. AllenAI released the dataset under the terms of ODC-BY. Users are also bound by the Common Crawl terms of use in respect of the content contained in the dataset. The full dataset can be cloned via git (downloads ~13TB to local drive).
datasetNoise: The original Common Crawl web extracted text from which C4 is derived largely comprises gibberish or boiler-plate text like menus, error messages, or duplicate text. A good deal of the scraped text contains content unlikely to be helpful, including offensive language, placeholder text, and source code. The en.noclean variant retains all such noise as no filtering heuristics were applied. The en variant applies heuristic filters to address these issues but residual noise may remain as the filters are heuristic-based.
datasetSize: Variant sizes: en: 305 GB (364,868,892 train rows + 364,608 validation rows); en.noclean: 2.3 TB; en.noblocklist: 380 GB; realnewslike: 15 GB; multilingual (mC4): 9.7 TB covering 108 languages. The base English C4 dataset (en) is described in the paper as "about 750 GB" of clean English text. Size category on Hugging Face: 10Bâ100B rows.
datasetType: Text dataset. Each example contains three fields: text (string â the web page content), url (string â the URL of the source page), timestamp (string â the timestamp of the snapshot). Files are stored in JSON.gz format (original) and auto-converted to Parquet on Hugging Face. The en, en.noclean, en.noblocklist, and realnewslike variants are in English. The multilingual (mC4) variant covers 108 languages. Languages that end with "-Latn" are romanized variants written using the Latin script. Tasks: text-generation, fill-mask. Sub-tasks: language-modeling, masked-language-modeling.
intendedUse: C4 and mC4 are mainly intended to pretrain language models and word representations. The dataset was originally created as a source of unlabeled text data for pre-training the T5 (Text-to-Text Transfer Transformer) model to achieve state-of-the-art results on NLP benchmarks including summarization, question answering, text classification, and machine translation.
knownBias: Not found

dataCollectionProcess: GSM8K consists of 8.5K high quality grade school math problems created by human problem writers. The authors avoided error-prone scraping procedures and instead relied on human workers to create problems. Data collection was performed by the team at Surge AI. Extensive quality control was performed based on workers' answer agreement; the authors estimate that less than 2% of problems contain breaking errors. The dataset was segmented into 7.5K training problems and 1K test problems. Additionally, a Socratic dataset variant was produced by automatically generating Socratic subquestions for each step using a model specifically finetuned for this task (on around 800 examples), then prefixing each step in the solution with the generated subquestion.
dataPreprocessing: Calculation annotations were injected into the training set to enable calculator use at test time. These annotations are strings that start with "<<" and end with ">>" (e.g., <<48/2=24>>). At training time, models finetune on this language data as-is; at test time, a calculator overrides sampling when the model chooses to use these annotations. To remove calculator annotations, any string starting with "<<" and ending with ">>" can be removed. The final numeric solution is the final line of the answer, preceded by "####". For the Socratic dataset variant, each step in the ground-truth solution was prefixed with a model-generated Socratic subquestion; steps were otherwise left untouched.
datasetAvailability: Publicly available on GitHub (openai/grade-school-math) and on Hugging Face (openai/gsm8k). License: MIT. Status: Archive â the GitHub repository is provided as-is, with no updates expected.
datasetNoise: After extensive quality control based on workers' answer agreement, the authors estimate that less than 2% of problems contain breaking errors. The provided model solution samples (example_model_solutions.jsonl) were generated with a slightly older version of the calculator; previous implementation bugs led to calculator failures in roughly 1% of model samples. Those issues have been fixed in the codebase, but since the samples have not been regenerated, occasional calculation errors are present in the provided model solutions.
datasetSize: 8,792 total rows on Hugging Face: 7,473 train rows and 1,319 test rows. The paper describes the full dataset as 8.5K problems: 7.5K training problems and 1K test problems. Two subsets are available: main and socratic, both with the same row counts. Size category on Hugging Face: 10Kâ100K.
datasetType: Text dataset, English, stored in Parquet format on Hugging Face (original files are JSONL). Each example contains two fields: "question" (a grade school math word problem) and "answer" (a natural language solution with embedded calculation annotations in <<...>> format, with the final numeric answer preceded by ####). Two subsets: "main" (standard solutions) and "socratic" (solutions prefixed with auto-generated Socratic subquestions). Tags: math-word-problems. Modalities: Text.
intendedUse: GSM8K is intended to diagnose the failures of current language models on multi-step mathematical reasoning and to support research in this area. Specifically, the dataset is designed for: (1) probing the informal reasoning ability of large language models; (2) training and evaluating verifiers that judge the correctness of model-generated solutions; (3) benchmarking finetuning approaches on grade school math problems. Problems require no concepts beyond the level of early Algebra, and solutions take between 2 and 8 steps using basic arithmetic operations (+ - Ã Ã·). A bright middle school student should be able to solve every problem.
knownBias: Not found

dataCollectionProcess: This HuggingFace repository serves as a file cache for the OSWorld benchmark, created by migrating evaluation files that were previously hosted on Google Drive (huggingface). The OSWorld benchmark tasks whose files are cached here were collected and annotated as follows: 369 real-world computer tasks on Ubuntu (and 43 for Windows) were collected by 9 computer science student authors over 3 months, consuming approximately 1,800 man-hours (650 hours on single-app tasks, 750 hours on workflow tasks, and 400 hours for double-checking) (arxiv). Task instructions and scenarios were sourced from: official guidelines and tutorials, video tutorials on YouTube and TikTok, how-to websites such as WikiHow, Q&A forums including Reddit, Quora, Superuser, and StackOverflow, formal video courses such as Coursera and Udemy, and publicly-available personal blogs and guidelines (arxiv). Examples were selected by judging their popularity, helpfulness, and diversity as revealed by views and votes. Each example was cross-checked by two other authors on feasibility, ambiguity, and alignment with the source (arxiv). The benchmark also includes 30 infeasible task examples that are inherently impossible to complete due to deprecated or hallucinated features raised by real users (arxiv).
dataPreprocessing: GitHub does not describe preprocessing steps specific to the file cache. From HuggingFace: the migration process from Google Drive to HuggingFace preserved the following: (1) File integrity â all files maintain their original content and format; (2) Original filenames are preserved where possible; (3) All OSWorld JSON configuration files have been updated to reference the new HuggingFace URLs; (4) Files are stored using HuggingFace's Git LFS (Large File Storage) system. Files are organized by application category mirroring the original OSWorld evaluation structure: chrome, firefox, gimp, libreoffice_calc, libreoffice_impress, libreoffice_writer, multi_apps, thunderbird, vscode, and others (huggingface). From arXiv: each task example's initial environment setup uses a hybrid configuration approach rather than solely relying on example-wise VM snapshots, divided into three stages: start the VM emulator, prepare files (download from cloud), and execute reprocessing commands (open files/tabs, change window size) (arxiv).
datasetAvailability: Publicly available. Code, environment, baseline models, and data are publicly available at https://os-world.github.io (arxiv). The file cache is hosted at HuggingFace (xlangai/ubuntu_osworld_file_cache) with public read access and no authentication required. License: Apache-2.0. Files are accessible directly via URL or programmatically via the HuggingFace Hub library (huggingface). Note: The 43 Windows tasks in the associated OSWorld benchmark require further activation by the user due to copyright issues (arxiv, github).
datasetNoise: Not found
datasetSize: The file cache hosts evaluation assets for 369 real-world computer tasks on Ubuntu and 43 tasks on Windows (arxiv). The benchmark contains 134 unique evaluation functions across all tasks (arxiv). Benchmark annotation took approximately 1,800 man-hours in total (arxiv). No total byte size is explicitly stated for the file cache repository itself. The cache had 338,913 downloads in the last month (huggingface).
datasetType: File cache repository â not a traditional structured dataset. Contains various file types used in OSWorld evaluations (huggingface): Images (screenshots, reference images, icons â PNG, JPG, GIF, etc.); Documents (text files, PDFs, spreadsheets, presentations); Media (audio and video files for multimedia evaluations); Data files (CSV files, JSON configurations, datasets); Archives (ZIP files containing multiple evaluation assets). Files are organized by application category: chrome, firefox, gimp, libreoffice_calc, libreoffice_impress, libreoffice_writer, multi_apps, thunderbird, vscode, and others. Stored using HuggingFace Git LFS (huggingface). From arXiv: observations in the associated benchmark include full desktop screenshots (including mouse position and shape) and XML-format accessibility (a11y) trees obtained via ATSPI2 on Ubuntu (arxiv).
datasetUpdateMechanism: Files are version-controlled using Git, ensuring reproducibility. Stored using HuggingFace's Git LFS (Large File Storage) system. If files are missing or inaccessible, users are directed to check the original OSWorld GitHub repository for the latest evaluation configurations and open an issue there (huggingface).
intendedUse: OSWorld is intended as a scalable, real computer environment for multimodal agents, supporting task setup, execution-based evaluation, and interactive learning across various operating systems (Ubuntu, Windows, macOS) (arxiv). Specifically, the benchmark and its associated file cache are designed for: (1) Benchmarking multimodal LLM/VLM-based agents on open-ended computer tasks involving arbitrary applications; (2) Diagnosing failures of current models in GUI grounding, operational knowledge, and multi-step reasoning; (3) Supporting research towards developing multimodal generalist computer agents; (4) Enabling interactive agent learning and real-world exploration in computer environments (arxiv). The benchmark covers 369 tasks on Ubuntu spanning real web and desktop apps (Chrome, Firefox, VLC, Thunderbird, VS Code, LibreOffice Calc/Writer/Impress, GIMP, and basic OS apps), OS file I/O, and multi-app workflows through both GUI and CLI (arxiv). This file cache repository specifically provides reliable and fast access to the evaluation files that OSWorld tasks depend on, previously hosted on Google Drive (huggingface).
knownBias: Not found

dataCollectionProcess: Questions were manually collected by graduate and undergraduate students from freely available sources online (arxiv). These include: practice questions for tests such as the Graduate Record Examination (GRE) and the United States Medical Licensing Examination (USMLE); questions designed for undergraduate courses; questions designed for readers of Oxford University Press books; freely available practice questions for the Examination for Professional Practice in Psychology (for the Professional Psychology task); questions like those from Advanced Placement Psychology examinations (for the High School Psychology task); and questions from forums, tutorials, guidelines, and other freely available online sources across 57 subject areas (arxiv). The 57 subjects span the humanities, social sciences, STEM, and other areas, ranging in difficulty from elementary to advanced professional level (arxiv).
dataPreprocessing: The collected questions were split into three sets: a few-shot development (dev) set with 5 questions per subject, a validation set of 1,540 questions, and a test set of 14,079 questions (arxiv). Each subject contains a minimum of 100 test examples (arxiv). Mathematics expressions are encoded using LaTeX or symbols such as * and ^ for multiplication and exponentiation respectively (arxiv). Questions are structured as multiple-choice with four answer options (A, B, C, D) (arxiv). On HuggingFace, each example contains four fields: question (string), subject (string), choices (sequence of 4 strings), and answer (class label: A/B/C/D) (huggingface). The dataset is organized into 59 subsets on HuggingFace, including an "all" subset and an "auxiliary_train" subset, with three splits: test, validation, and dev (huggingface).
datasetAvailability: Publicly available. The test and code are available at github.com/hendrycks/test (arxiv). The test data is available for download at https://people.eecs.berkeley.edu/~hendrycks/data.tar (github). Also available on HuggingFace as cais/mmlu, auto-converted to Parquet format. License: MIT (huggingface, github).
datasetNoise: Not found
datasetSize: 15,908 questions total, split into: a few-shot dev set (5 questions per subject Ã 57 subjects = 285 questions); a validation set of 1,540 questions; and a test set of 14,079 questions (arxiv). On HuggingFace (cais/mmlu), the dataset has a size category of 100Kâ1M rows. The "all" subset contains approximately 116K rows and the "auxiliary_train" subset contains 99.8K rows (huggingface).
datasetType: Text dataset in English, stored in Parquet format on HuggingFace (huggingface). Task type: Question Answering / multiple-choice-qa (huggingface). Each example is a multiple-choice question with exactly 4 answer choices (A, B, C, D) and a ground-truth answer label (huggingface, arxiv). The dataset covers 57 subjects spanning four broad areas: humanities, social sciences, STEM, and other (arxiv). Difficulty ranges from elementary level to advanced professional level (arxiv). Subjects include elementary mathematics, US history, computer science, law, medicine, philosophy, economics, psychology, and more (arxiv). Available in 59 subsets on HuggingFace, including per-subject subsets and an "all" aggregated subset (huggingface).
intendedUse: The benchmark is designed to measure a text model's multitask accuracy across 57 subjects (arxiv). Specifically, it is intended to: (1) measure knowledge acquired during pretraining by evaluating models exclusively in zero-shot and few-shot settings; (2) comprehensively evaluate the breadth and depth of a model's academic and professional understanding; (3) analyze models across many tasks and identify important shortcomings; (4) serve as an enduring and informative goalpost for future models that should be well-rounded, possess extensive world knowledge, and develop expert-level problem solving ability (arxiv). The test and code are provided for model evaluation purposes (github).
knownBias: Models evaluated on MMLU exhibit lopsided performance â they do not perform uniformly across subjects (arxiv). Both GPT-3 and UnifiedQA are below expert-level performance for all 57 tasks (arxiv). Calculation-heavy STEM subjects tend to have lower accuracy compared to verbal subjects; 9 out of the 10 lowest-accuracy tasks for GPT-3 are STEM subjects emphasizing mathematics or calculations (arxiv). Some socially important verbal subjects such as morality and law also have near-random accuracy (arxiv). GPT-3 acquires knowledge in a pedagogically unusual order â for example, it performs better on College Medicine (47.4%) and College Mathematics (35.0%) than on Elementary Mathematics (29.9%) (arxiv). GPT-3 is also miscalibrated: its confidence can be off from its actual accuracy by up to 24% (arxiv). Unspecialized humans from Amazon Mechanical Turk obtain only 34.5% accuracy on the test (arxiv).

dataPreprocessing: Extensive filtering pipeline documented (min sentence/word thresholds, keyword removal, deduplication, pct_questionable scoring, virama correction, Zawgyi conversion, Chinese porn blocklist)
