anonymizationMethodUsed: Personal Identifiable Information (PII) removal was applied by anonymizing email addresses and public IP addresses.
dataCollectionProcess: FineWeb-Edu is a subset of FineWeb, which was derived from 96 Common Crawl snapshots. Common Crawl is a publicly available and continually updated collection of website snapshots that has been running since 2007. Text was extracted directly from WARC (Web ARChive format) files â which contain the raw data from the crawl including full page HTML and request metadata â using the open source trafilatura library, rather than from WET (WARC Encapsulated Text) files, because WET files were found to retain too much boilerplate and menu text. To build synthetic annotations for the educational classifier, Llama-3-70B-Instruct was used to score 460,000 randomly sampled webpages from the FineWeb CC-MAIN-2024-10 snapshot for their educational quality on a scale from 0 to 5.
dataPreprocessing:
The full pipeline applied to produce FineWeb (from which FineWeb-Edu is filtered) is:
1. Text extraction: WARC files processed with trafilatura library (preferred over WET files).
2. Base filtering: URL filtering using a blocklist to remove adult content; fastText language classifier to keep only English text with score >= 0.65; quality and repetition filters from MassiveText using original thresholds. This yielded ~36 trillion tokens.
3. Deduplication: Individual per-crawl MinHash deduplication (independently per snapshot). Parameters: 5-grams using an English word tokenizer, 112 hash functions split into 14 buckets of 8 hashes each, targeting documents at least 75% similar. Transitive clustering applied; one randomly chosen document kept per duplicate cluster. This yielded 20 trillion tokens.
4. C4 filters (all except terminal punctuation filter): removing lines mentioning javascript or with "terms-of-use"/"cookie policy" statements; dropping documents too short or containing "lorem ipsum" or curly brackets ({).
5. Custom heuristic filters: documents removed where fraction of lines ending with punctuation <= 0.12 (10.14% of tokens removed); fraction of characters in duplicated lines >= 0.1 (12.47% of tokens removed); fraction of lines shorter than 30 characters >= 0.67 (3.73% of tokens removed). Combined ~22% of tokens removed.
6. PII removal: email and public IP addresses anonymized.
Result: FineWeb â 15 trillion tokens from 96 Common Crawl snapshots.
For FineWeb-Edu specifically:
7. Educational classifier training: linear regression model on top of Snowflake-arctic-embed-m embedding model (embedding and encoder layers kept frozen), fine-tuned on 410,000 Llama-3-70B-Instruct synthetic annotations for 20 epochs with learning rate 3e-4. Validated on held-out 50,000 samples; F1 score of 82% on validation set. Output scores rounded to integers 0â5.
8. Threshold filtering: minimum score of 3 applied to FineWeb to produce FineWeb-Edu. Applying classifier to 15 trillion tokens required 6,000 H100 GPU hours.
datasetAvailability: Publicly available on HuggingFace at https://huggingface.co/datasets/HuggingFaceFW/fineweb-edu. Released under the ODC-By (Open Data Commons Attribution) License. The data curation codebase (datatrove) and all models trained during ablation experiments are also publicly released.
datasetNoise: Web text can contain a large amount of "unnatural" language such as boilerplate text and gibberish. Web text can also contain a large amount of duplicated content. Global deduplication of older snapshots was found to upsample lower-quality data: originally kept data (after global dedup) contained more ads, incoherent lists of keywords, and generally badly formatted text than the originally removed data. The educational classifier may introduce topic skew: it heavily favors topics such as Education/Learning/Teaching and History/Culture/Politics, while down-sampling Business/Finance/Law, Entertainment/Film/Theater, and Places/Travel/Real Estate.
datasetSize: FineWeb-Edu: 1.3 trillion tokens. Parent dataset FineWeb: 15 trillion tokens from 96 Common Crawl snapshots. HuggingFace default subset: 1.53 billion rows. HuggingFace size category: 1Bâ10B. Individual per-crawl subsets range from approximately 7.4M to 22.9M rows. Applying the educational classifier to the 15 trillion tokens of FineWeb required 6,000 H100 GPU hours.
datasetType: Text dataset. English only (monolingual). Web-sourced educational text filtered from Common Crawl. Format on HuggingFace: Parquet. Modalities: Tabular and Text. Task: Text Generation. Each record contains: text (string), id (string), dump (string), url (string), date (string), file_path (string), language (string), language_score (float), token_count (int), score (float), int_score (int).
hasSensitivePersonalInformation: PII was present in the raw data. For the public release, Personal Identifiable Information (PII) removal was applied by anonymizing email addresses and public IP addresses.
intendedUse: FineWeb-Edu is intended for use as a large-scale LLM pretraining dataset, specifically optimized for educational content. LLMs pretrained on FineWeb-Edu exhibit dramatically better performance on knowledge- and reasoning-intensive benchmarks like MMLU and ARC compared to models trained on other open pretraining datasets. The dataset is designed to advance public knowledge and resources for curating LLM pre-training datasets.
knownBias: The FineWeb dataset (parent) has a relative overrepresentation of words reflecting hegemonic norms known to be overrepresented in online text, such as "man" and "christian". The most skewed association found was between religion words and intimacy (e.g., "christian dating", "jewish singles"). The FineWeb-Edu educational classifier heavily favors topics such as "Education, Learning, Teaching" and "History, Culture, Politics", while down-sampling "Business, Finance, Law", "Entertainment, Film, Theater", and "Places, Travel, Real Estate". FineWeb-Edu captures associations more expected from educational content, e.g., "man" associated to "king" and "woman" associated to "pregnancy". Both datasets are entirely comprised of English text (noted as a path to further improvement in the paper).

dataCollectionProcess: The dataset is fully synthetically generated — it does not involve collection from human subjects or web scraping. Nine different text-only instruction-tuned language models (ranging from 4B to 72B parameters) are paired to generate multi-turn dialogues: Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct, phi4-mini-instruct, phi-4, c4ai-command-r7b, gemma3-4B-Instruct, and gemma3-27B-Instruct. 14 different model pairs are obtained, including both same-model pairings and cross-model interactions. A stratified sampling approach is used to ensure balanced representation across 41 domains and 20 emotions. Each dialogue covers between 3 and 10 turns; models are instructed to maintain domain relevance while expressing specific emotions via few-shot prompting. A maximum response length of 25 words per turn is enforced. This process yielded 65,600 initial raw dialogues (1,600 per domain). For the speech component, the XTTS variant uses XTTS-v2 with reference audio samples from the RAVDESS dataset (recordings of 24 actors expressing 8 emotional states) for emotion conditioning. The Orpheus variant uses Orpheus TTS with 8 distinct pre-defined voices, relying on linguistic cues for emotion.
dataPreprocessing: A multi-stage quality evaluation and filtering pipeline was applied to the 65,600 raw generated dialogues: (1) Human annotation: a subset of 984 dialogues (24 per domain, 123 per number of turns) was rated by three independent human annotators on dialogue coherence, emotional consistency, and domain relevance using a Likert scale from 1 to 5 and a binary validity score. Inter-annotator agreement was measured using Fleiss kappa, achieving κ = 0.80. (2) Automated LLM-based filtering: an ensemble of open-source models (Gemma-3-27B-Instruct, Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct) was used to evaluate all 65,600 dialogues. Only dialogues with scores >= 3 on all dimensions and a positive binary judgment were retained. (3) Safety filtering: each dialogue was passed through Llama Guard, a safety-focused LLM classifier; only dialogues passing its safety filters were included. (4) This filtering process yielded 40,150 high-quality dialogues (approximately 61% of the initial generation). For speech synthesis preprocessing: text was preprocessed by removing non-speech symbols and emojis while preserving punctuation. For the XTTS variant, the 20 emotional states were mapped to RAVDESS's 8 emotions (e.g., "excited," "amused," and "enthusiastic" mapped to "happy"; "anxious" mapped to "fearful"; "disappointed" mapped to "disgust"). For each actor-emotion combination, the two standard RAVDESS sentences were concatenated to create reference audio samples, using only normal intensity recordings rather than strong intensity.
datasetAvailability: Publicly available on HuggingFace. The FDlalala/tranS repository on HuggingFace is the XTTS-v2 variant. License on HuggingFace dataset card: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International License); HuggingFace metadata also lists cc-by-nc-4.0. The full XTTS dataset is also available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/SALT-Research/DeepDialogue-xtts (~180GB). The Orpheus variant is available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/SALT-Research/DeepDialogue-orpheus. The dataset is large (~180GB) due to the inclusion of high-quality audio files; cloning requires Git LFS. GitHub code repository: https://github.com/SALT-Research/DeepDialogue-code (no license stated for the code repo).
datasetNoise: The paper explicitly identifies the following noise and quality issues: (1) The dominant failure modes in filtered-out dialogues are hallucination and irrelevance, often co-occurring with domain mismatch. Emotion-related inconsistencies appear more frequently in domains like "Relationships" and "Celebrities." (2) Longer conversations (9-10 turns) exhibit a higher rate of failure, with smaller models increasingly deviating from instructions, losing emotional consistency, and straying from the original domain. (3) The synthetic nature of the dataset, despite rigorous quality filtering, may differ from authentic human interactions, especially in longer conversations where maintaining coherence becomes difficult even for state-of-the-art models. (4) Current speech synthesis technology cannot replicate all the subtle vocal characteristics of human emotional expression, such as spontaneous voice breaks, natural hesitations, and physiological effects of genuine emotions on vocal production.
datasetSize: The final DeepDialogue dataset contains 40,150 high-quality multi-turn dialogues with a total of 241,825 turns and 488 hours of audio, spanning 41 domains and 20 distinct emotions. The mean conversation length is 6.1 turns. The speech component for each variant (XTTS-v2 and Orpheus) contains utterances from all 40,150 conversations, totaling over 480 hours of audio across more than 240,000 individual turns. The full XTTS dataset is approximately 180GB. On HuggingFace (FDlalala/tranS): size category 100K-1M; 243,295 rows; 80.2 MB of downloaded dataset files. The dataset has a single train split.
datasetType: Large-scale multimodal dataset containing multi-turn dialogues with paired text and synthesized speech. HuggingFace task categories: Audio Classification, Automatic Speech Recognition. Modalities: Audio, Tabular, Text. Format: Parquet. Language: English. Tags: conversational, emotions, dialogues, conversations. The FDlalala/tranS repository is the XTTS-v2 variant, where speech is generated using XTTS-v2 with explicit emotional conditioning. The dataset includes multi-turn dialogues (3-10 turns) between two AI agents, domain-specific content across 41 topics, emotional annotations for each utterance (20 distinct emotions), high-quality synthesized speech with explicit emotion conditioning, and paired audio-text data. Data is organized into dialogue JSON files, full audio WAV files, per-segment WAV files, and metadata TSV files. A companion dataset using Orpheus TTS (DeepDialogue-orpheus) is also available.
hasSensitivePersonalInformation: The paper explicitly states: "Our dataset consists entirely of synthetic dialogues rather than recordings of real human conversations, which eliminates direct privacy concerns associated with human data collection." Emotional speech synthesis is noted to present concerns regarding potential misuse for creating misleading content designed to elicit specific emotional responses.
intendedUse: DeepDialogue is designed to support research in conversational AI, specifically: (1) enabling research on emotionally intelligent conversational systems with explicit emotional annotations and transitions; (2) comprehensive analysis of domain- and model-specific dialogue patterns and challenges across 41 domains, 14 different model combinations, and 7 turn types; (3) bridging text-based dialogue research and real-world spoken conversation applications via emotionally expressive multimodal dialogue data; (4) novel research in dialogue understanding, speech-language model integration, and emotion-aware spoken systems; (5) training models for speech emotion recognition (SER). The GitHub code repository specifically hosts code for the SER task. Future work noted by the authors includes leveraging the resource to fine-tune dialogue models to enhance emotional intelligence and conversational coherence, develop more sophisticated emotion transition models, and extend the framework to additional languages and cultural contexts.
knownBias: The paper explicitly states the following biases and limitations: (1) Representation bias: "the synthetic dialogues inevitably reflect biases present in the underlying language models' training data." "The dataset inherently reflects the distribution patterns of the underlying language models, which, despite our diverse domain selection, may not equally represent all cultural contexts or conversational styles." (2) Model size bias: smaller models (4-8B parameters) fail to maintain coherence beyond 6 dialogue turns and produce substantially more invalid dialogues, particularly when paired with themselves. Pairings involving Command-r7B or Gemma3-4B achieve the lowest acceptance rates. (3) Domain type bias: concrete domains (e.g., "cars," "travel," "cooking") consistently yield more grounded and meaningful conversations compared to abstract domains (e.g., "philosophy," "spirituality"), as measured by Brysbaert concreteness ratings (concrete mean score 3.01 vs. abstract mean 2.21). (4) Cross-model effect: dialogues generated by pairing different models (acceptance rate 0.65) consistently outperform same-model pairings (acceptance rate 0.54). (5) Emotion taxonomy simplification: the emotion taxonomy represents a practical simplification of human emotional expression, which in reality exists on continuous spectra rather than as discrete categories.

dataCollectionProcess: GLUE is a collection of pre-existing NLU datasets; none of the datasets in GLUE were created from scratch for the benchmark. The constituent tasks and their sources are as follows: (1) CoLA (Corpus of Linguistic Acceptability): English acceptability judgments drawn from books and journal articles on linguistic theory. (2) SST-2 (Stanford Sentiment Treebank): sentences from movie reviews with human annotations of their sentiment. (3) MRPC (Microsoft Research Paraphrase Corpus): sentence pairs automatically extracted from online news sources, with human annotations for semantic equivalence. (4) QQP (Quora Question Pairs): question pairs collected from the community question-answering website Quora. (5) STS-B (Semantic Textual Similarity Benchmark): sentence pairs drawn from news headlines, video and image captions, and natural language inference data, each human-annotated with a similarity score from 1 to 5. (6) MNLI (Multi-Genre Natural Language Inference): a crowdsourced collection of sentence pairs with textual entailment annotations; premise sentences gathered from ten different sources including transcribed speech, fiction, and government reports. (7) QNLI: derived from the Stanford Question Answering Dataset (SQuAD), which consists of question-paragraph pairs where one sentence contains the answer, drawn from Wikipedia, with questions written by annotators. Converted into sentence pair classification by pairing each question with each context sentence and filtering out pairs with low lexical overlap. (8) RTE (Recognizing Textual Entailment): combined data from RTE1 (Dagan et al., 2006), RTE2 (Bar Haim et al., 2006), RTE3 (Giampiccolo et al., 2007), and RTE5 (Bentivogli et al., 2009); examples constructed based on news and Wikipedia text. (9) WNLI (Winograd NLI): derived from the Winograd Schema Challenge (Levesque et al., 2011); small evaluation set of new examples derived from fiction books, shared privately by the original corpus authors. (10) AX (Diagnostic dataset): a manually-curated set of NLI sentence pairs hand-crafted by the benchmark authors to highlight specific linguistic phenomena; examples based on naturally-occurring sentences from news, Reddit, Wikipedia, and academic papers. Four of the datasets feature privately-held test data to ensure fair use of the benchmark.
dataPreprocessing: For individual tasks, preprocessing steps include: (1) QNLI: task converted from question answering into sentence pair classification by forming a pair between each question and each sentence in the corresponding context, and filtering out pairs with low lexical overlap (using CBoW representations with pre-trained GloVe embeddings as the similarity metric). To construct a balanced dataset, all pairs in which the most similar sentence to the question was not the answer sentence were selected, plus an equal number of cases in which the correct sentence was most similar but another distracting sentence was a close second. (2) RTE: all datasets converted to a two-class split, where for three-class datasets neutral and contradiction are collapsed into not entailment, for consistency. (3) WNLI: sentence pairs constructed by replacing the ambiguous pronoun with each possible referent. (4) Diagnostic dataset: each source sentence located and edited in two ways to produce an appropriate sentence pair; minimal modifications made to maintain high lexical and structural overlap; each pair labeled considering each sentence alternatively as the premise, producing two labeled examples per pair (1,100 total). The dataset was audited for annotation artifacts by training two fastText classifiers to predict entailment labels using only the hypothesis as input; models achieved near-chance accuracies of 32.7% and 36.4%. The GitHub repository (DEPRECATED) provides a convenience Python script (download_glue_data.py) for downloading all GLUE data and standard splits.
datasetAvailability: Publicly available on HuggingFace at nyu-mll/glue. License on HuggingFace: "other". The benchmark and evaluation platform are hosted at https://gluebenchmark.com/. Four of the datasets feature privately-held test data; to evaluate on the private test data, users must submit to gluebenchmark.com. The GitHub repository (https://github.com/nyu-mll/GLUE-baselines) is marked as DEPRECATED; a newer version of the baseline code is available at https://github.com/jsalt18-sentence-repl/jiant. A convenience Python script (download_glue_data.py) is provided in the GitHub repo for downloading all GLUE data and standard splits. The GLUE website limits users to two submissions per day to avoid overfitting to the private test data.
datasetNoise: The paper explicitly identifies the following quality and noise issues: (1) Annotation artifacts: the benchmark authors audited the diagnostic dataset for artifacts that can be exploited to perform well without solving the intended task, following the methodology of Gururangan et al. (2018). Two fastText classifiers trained using only the hypothesis achieved near-chance accuracies of 32.7% and 36.4%, showing the diagnostic data does not suffer from such artifacts. (2) WNLI data quirk: the development set is adversarial — hypotheses are sometimes shared between training and development examples, so a model that memorizes training examples will predict the wrong label on corresponding development examples. The test set is imbalanced (65% not entailment). (3) QQP label distribution mismatch: the test set has a different label distribution than the training set (63% negative in training). (4) MRPC class imbalance: classes are imbalanced (68% positive). (5) QNLI earlier version artifact: an earlier release of QNLI had an artifact where the task could be modeled and solved as an easier task; an updated version was released to remove this.
datasetSize: HuggingFace size category: 1M–10M rows. The benchmark comprises 12 subsets on HuggingFace. Per-task train/test sizes as reported in the paper: CoLA: 8.5k train, 1k test; SST-2: 67k train, 1.8k test; MRPC: 3.7k train, 1.7k test; STS-B: 7k train, 1.4k test; QQP: 364k train, 391k test; MNLI: 393k train, 20k test; QNLI: 105k train, 5.4k test; RTE: 2.5k train, 3k test; WNLI: 634 train, 146 test. HuggingFace dataset split sizes: ax test: 1,104; cola: 8,551 train / 1,043 validation / 1,063 test; mnli: 392,702 train / 9,815 validation_matched / 9,832 validation_mismatched / 9,796 test_matched / 9,847 test_mismatched. File sizes: ax: 0.22 MB downloaded / 0.24 MB generated; mnli: 312.78 MB downloaded / 82.47 MB generated.
datasetType: A collection of nine English sentence or sentence-pair NLU tasks plus one diagnostic dataset, covering a diverse range of text genres, dataset sizes, and degrees of difficulty. HuggingFace task category: Text Classification. Sub-tasks: acceptability-classification, natural-language-inference, semantic-similarity-scoring, paraphrase-identification, question-answering NLI. Modalities: Tabular, Text. Format: Parquet (auto-converted on HuggingFace). Language: English (BCP-47 en). Tags: qa-nli, coreference-nli, paraphrase-identification. All tasks are single sentence or sentence pair classification, except STS-B which is a regression task. MNLI has three classes; all other classification tasks have two. The diagnostic dataset (AX) is a manually-curated NLI test set for fine-grained linguistic analysis, not intended as a benchmark.
datasetUpdateMechanism: The benchmark is hosted online at https://gluebenchmark.com/. The GLUE website limits users to two submissions per day to avoid overfitting to the private test data. An earlier release of QNLI had an artifact and was replaced by an updated version removing this possibility. The GitHub baseline code repository is marked as DEPRECATED; newer development is directed to https://github.com/jsalt18-sentence-repl/jiant.
hasSensitivePersonalInformation: The HuggingFace dataset card section "Personal and Sensitive Information" states: "More Information Needed," indicating this was not explicitly addressed in the dataset card. No explicit mention of sensitive personal information is made in the arXiv paper or GitHub repository.
intendedUse: GLUE is designed as a benchmark and analysis platform for evaluating the performance of models across a diverse set of existing NLU tasks. It is designed to favor and encourage models that share general linguistic knowledge across tasks. By including tasks with limited training data, GLUE is designed to favor models that can learn to represent linguistic knowledge in a way that facilitates sample-efficient learning and effective knowledge-transfer across tasks. GLUE includes a hand-crafted diagnostic test suite that enables detailed linguistic analysis of models. The diagnostic dataset is provided not as a benchmark but as an analysis tool for error analysis, qualitative model comparison, and development of adversarial examples. The online evaluation platform and leaderboard, based primarily on privately-held test data, is model-agnostic and can evaluate any method capable of producing results on all nine tasks. The GitHub repository contains baseline code for exploring multi-task learning approaches to learning sentence representations.
knownBias: The paper explicitly identifies the following biases and limitations: (1) Annotation artifacts in crowdsourced NLI data: following Gururangan et al. (2018), Poliak et al. (2018), Tsuchiya (2018), crowdsourced data often contains artifacts that can be exploited to perform well without solving the intended task. The diagnostic dataset was specifically audited for such artifacts. (2) WNLI development set adversarial quirk: hypotheses are sometimes shared between training and development examples, causing models that memorize training examples to predict the wrong label on corresponding development examples. (3) MNLI test labels: four of the datasets feature privately-held test data. (4) Models relying on lexical cues: diagnostic analysis reveals that baseline models deal well with strong lexical signals but struggle with deeper logical structure, particularly double negation, downward monotonicity, and restrictivity. Models were found to be sensitive to hypernym/hyponym substitution and word deletion as a signal of entailment, but predict it in the wrong direction (as if in an upward monotone context), consistent with findings by McCoy & Linzen (2019) that these systems use the subsequence relation between premise and hypothesis as a heuristic shortcut. (5) Tasks heavily skewed toward sentiment analysis in related benchmarks were deliberately excluded to avoid this bias in GLUE.

anonymizationMethodUsed: The paper explicitly states that the newly contributed head CT scans are anonymized: "484 newly acquired anonymized head CTs." No further description of the anonymization method is provided in any of the sources.
dataCollectionProcess: The CADS-dataset aggregates 22,022 CT volumes from 40 diverse sources. Data was collected from three main source types: (1) Large-scale public archives: TotalSegmentator (1,203 annotated volumes covering 104 anatomical structures), National Lung Screening Trial (NLST, 7,172 scans), CT-RATE (3,134 scans), and AbdomenCT-1K (1,062 scans). (2) Specialized annotation collections from medical imaging challenges via Grand Challenge and MICCAI, and focused research studies such as HaN-Seg (for head-and-neck OARs), SAROS (for broad body context structures), and various oncology studies from TCIA (The Cancer Imaging Archive). (3) Newly contributed images and annotations: manual annotations of critical OARs on the VISCERAL dataset, 484 newly acquired anonymized head CTs, and 586 triphasic contrast-enhanced abdominal CT scans. The dataset spans more than 100 imaging facilities (also stated as 100+ imaging centers) across 16 countries, collected between 2007 and 2024. Data includes both publicly available datasets and private hospital data. Coverage includes healthy subjects and various pathological conditions: oncological diseases (carcinomas, lymphomas, metastases), COVID-19, traumatic injuries, and rare disorders. Imaging protocols include contrast-enhanced and non-contrast acquisitions, with multiple fields of view: whole-body, head-and-neck, chest, abdomen, pelvis, and spine. Of the 22,022 CT volumes, only 4,695 (21%) originally contained partial annotations from public sources.
dataPreprocessing: The annotation methodology involves the following steps: (1) Data standardization: all images aligned to consistent orientation and 1.5 mm isotropic resolution. (2) The 167 target structures are organized into nine anatomical groups; specialized models are developed for each group leveraging high-quality annotations from established datasets. (3) Pseudo-labeling (step 1): specialized models perform whole-body pseudo-labeling to propagate annotations across remaining unlabeled scans. (4) Automated outlier detection (step 2): automated outlier detection of pseudo-labels in shape space using neural implicit functions. (5) Multi-strategy label generation (step 3): ensemble selection mechanism that optimizes structure-specific segmentation strategies by combining complementary models trained with different data quality-quantity trade-offs. (6) Structure-specific quality controls and refinements, including a rib segmentation correction process that accurately preserves costovertebral joints. HuggingFace notes a post-release update (2025-10-04): fixed missing images and corrected affine/intensity errors in datasets 0010_verse, 0041_ctrate, and 0043_new_ct_tri.
datasetAvailability: The dataset is publicly available on HuggingFace. The primary HuggingFace repository is mrmrx/CADS-dataset; sunghong/CADS-dataset is a duplicate of this. HuggingFace license listed as "cadsdataset" (a customized license). Each constituent dataset subdirectory contains its own README.md with individual dataset license information. Per-source licenses include: CC BY 4.0, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0, CC BY-SA 4.0, CC BY 3.0, CC BY-NC 4.0, CC BY-NC-ND 4.0, CC BY-NC 3.0, TCIA Data Usage Policy, and customized licenses for some sources. The HuggingFace dataset card states: "We are not the original owners of the CT images, except for the BrainCT-1mm and CT-TRI datasets newly released in this project." Users should review the README.md in each dataset subdirectory before use. The CADS-model codebase (GitHub) is licensed under Apache License 2.0. CADS-model weights are licensed under CC BY 4.0. All annotations are publicly available in their original image formats. The paper states the dataset, segmentation models, and clinical software tool are all made publicly available.
datasetNoise: The paper explicitly identifies the following noise and quality issues: (1) Existing automated labeling approaches introduce known systematic errors in complex anatomical structures like ribs and vertebrae; the paper states: "Our evaluation on the TotalSegmentator dataset reveals inaccuracies in these ground-truth labels across many structures, particularly evident in ribs and vertebrae segmentations." To avoid these concerns in the CADS-dataset, an expert-verified subset was created for more reliable benchmarking. (2) Challenges with 48 new target structures: limited training data for certain structures (e.g., buccal mucosa appears in fewer than 1,000 scans with only 30 ground-truth annotations, compared to well-represented structures in over 10,000 scans with several hundred ground-truth annotations); very small organs (volumes below 0.5 mL) where standard CT resolution limits and partial volume effects impact accuracy (e.g., arytenoid cartilage); and anatomically complex structures, particularly small glands. (3) Only 21% of the 22,022 source CT volumes originally contained any annotations, with annotations typically confined to a single organ system; the remaining 79% were unannotated and required pseudo-labeling. (4) HuggingFace update (2025-10-04) notes: missing images and affine/intensity errors were found and corrected in datasets 0010_verse, 0041_ctrate, and 0043_new_ct_tri.
datasetSize: 22,022 CT volumes with complete voxel-level annotations for 167 anatomical structures. HuggingFace reports 22,011 rows across 40 subsets and size category 10K–100K. Auto-converted Parquet files on HuggingFace: 835 kB. The dataset contains 40 subsets (directories), the largest being: NLST (7,172 scans), CT-RATE (3,134 scans), STOIC2021 (2,000), TotalSegmentator (1,203), AbdomenCT-1K (1,062), LIDC-IDRI (997), SAROS (900), KiTS21 (300). Newly contributed data: 484 head CTs (BrainCT-1mm) and 586 triphasic contrast-enhanced abdominal CTs (CT-TRI). The paper states this is 18 times more CT scans and 60% more distinct anatomical targets than existing collections. All images and segmentations are provided in NIfTI format.
datasetType: Large-scale medical CT imaging dataset for whole-body anatomical segmentation. HuggingFace task category: Image Segmentation. Modalities: Tabular, Text, Image (also includes 3D volumes). Format: NIfTI (.nii.gz), with CSV metadata; auto-converted to Parquet on HuggingFace. Tags: medical, ct, segmentation, image, 3d, whole-body. Contains original CT volumes and corresponding segmentation masks organized by data source. Directory structure: root / dataset_name / images (original CT volumes) / segmentations (segmentation masks) / README.md. Includes varying imaging protocols (contrast-enhanced and non-contrast acquisitions) and multiple anatomical fields of view: whole-body, head-and-neck, chest, abdomen, pelvis, and spine. Covers both healthy subjects and pathological conditions including oncological diseases, COVID-19, traumatic injuries, and rare disorders. The dataset is a multi-source collection aggregating data from 40 distinct sources.
datasetUpdateMechanism: HuggingFace dataset card explicitly notes: "Update (2025-10-04): Fixed missing images and corrected affine/intensity errors in datasets 0010_verse, 0041_ctrate, and 0043_new_ct_tri, see details for affected IDs." No further update mechanism is described in any of the sources.
hasSensitivePersonalInformation: The dataset contains clinical patient CT scans including oncology patients (kidney and liver tumors, lung cancer, other malignancies), COVID-19 patients, trauma cases, and other pathological conditions. The paper states that newly acquired head CTs are anonymized: "484 newly acquired anonymized head CTs." The dataset includes data from over 100 medical centers and a real-world hospital cohort of 2,864 oncology patients from the Radiation Oncology Department at University Hospital Zurich. No explicit statement is made in any source about whether sensitive personal information is present or absent in the publicly released data beyond the anonymization note for the newly acquired head CTs.
intendedUse: The CADS-dataset is described as serving dual purposes: (1) enabling development of the whole-body segmentation CADS-model; (2) supporting further research in medical imaging. Clinical applications explicitly mentioned in the paper include: precise radiation planning in radiation oncology; region-specific abnormality detection to support rapid diagnosis, triage, and mortality risk assessment; automated localization of metastases; body composition analysis through tissue quantification. Technical research directions mentioned: anatomical landmark detection, cross-modality registration, anatomy-guided synthesis. Clinical research directions mentioned: longitudinal organ tracking, personalized anatomical modeling, radiation treatment planning, and large-scale population studies. The paper explicitly states the goal is to "advance robust AI solutions in radiology and make comprehensive anatomical analysis accessible to clinicians and researchers alike." The CADS-model is provided as a 3D Slicer plugin designed for both clinicians and researchers, offering simple installation, one-click inference, and segmentation results presented according to the SNOMED-CT terminology standard. GitHub describes the repo as: "Whole-body CT segmentation made simple: 22,022 scans, 167 structures, one open solution."
knownBias: The paper explicitly identifies the following limitations and biases: (1) Annotation coverage bias: only 21% of the 22,022 source CT volumes originally contained any annotations, with annotations typically confined to a single organ system. (2) Inconsistent annotation quality in existing public datasets: the paper states that "existing automated labeling approaches introduce known systematic errors in complex anatomical structures like ribs and vertebrae," and identifies inaccuracies in TotalSegmentator ground-truth labels particularly in ribs and vertebrae, with the risk that "models trained or evaluated on such data" may propagate these errors. (3) Limited data for certain new structures: buccal mucosa appears in fewer than 1,000 scans with only 30 ground-truth annotations, compared to well-represented structures in over 10,000 scans with several hundred ground-truth annotations. (4) Small organ limitations: very small organs (volumes below 0.5 mL) present accuracy challenges due to standard CT resolution limits and partial volume effects. (5) The paper notes that "models developed on small-scale or single-source datasets show limited generalizability across institutions, populations, and imaging protocols" as a known limitation of prior approaches that CADS aims to address. (6) Some areas present challenges for the model: brain mask definition and vessel delineation were identified by expert review as areas with opportunities for improvement.
sensorUsed: Computed Tomography (CT) scanners. The paper explicitly states the dataset consists of CT volumes. The paper mentions "diverse scanners, protocols" and data spanning "100+ imaging facilities across 16 countries" with "varying imaging protocols (contrast-enhanced and non-contrast acquisitions)." No specific scanner models or calibration values are mentioned in any of the sources.

dataCollectionProcess: ManyPeptidesMD is a fully synthetic/computational dataset; no human subjects or real-world measurements involved. All simulations were performed using OpenMM (Eastman et al., 2017) with the amber-14 forcefield (amber14-all.xml, implicit/obc1.xml). Simulation configuration as specified in the HuggingFace dataset card: nonbondedMethod = CutoffNonPeriodic; nonbondedCutoff = 2.0 nanometers; temperature = 310 Kelvin; integrator = LangevinMiddleIntegrator with friction 0.3 ps^-1 and timestep 1.0 femtosecond; constraints = None. For training: 21,700 uniformly sampled peptide sequences (lengths 2–8 residues) each simulated for 200 ns; positions and velocities saved every 1 ps. For validation & testing: sequences randomly sampled such that all amino acids are represented equally, simulated for 5 µs; positions and velocities saved every 10 ps. The paper states: "Following Klein et al. [2023a] all simulation is performed using OpenMM with the amber-14 forcefield." Computational resources were provided by the Digital Research Alliance of Canada, Mila, and NVIDIA.
dataPreprocessing: The dataset is provided in multiple formats with the following preprocessing applied: (1) Webdataset (path: webdatasets/single_frames/): each .tar contains 4 randomly selected position frames per sequence, from a 10 ps/frame subsample of the full training trajectories. Samples are preshuffled within each .tar. Sample filenames formatted as {SEQUENCE}_{TIME}.bin where {TIME} is the time in picoseconds from the original trajectory. (2) Subsampled validation & test sets (path: trajectories_subsampled/): 500 ps downsample of validation/testing trajectories, giving sets of size 10,000 as used in the paper's evaluation. Includes TICA projection data computed from full 10 ps interval trajectories for metric calculation. (3) Post-release correction (uploaded 15/12/2025): the original 8AA TICA models within subsampled_trajectories/*/8AA/*.npz employed a CA-only atom selection and were invalid; corrected models were uploaded to replace them. Codebase references to tica_features_ca must be replaced with tica_features; resolved in the codebase by PR #26. Unguarded snapshot_download calls automatically redownload relevant files when a change in the repo is detected.
datasetAvailability: Publicly available on HuggingFace at transferable-samplers/many-peptides-md. HuggingFace license: MIT. The paper states the codebase, model weights, and training dataset are all open-sourced: "We open source our codebase https://github.com/transferable-samplers/transferable-samplers, ManyPeptidesMD dataset https://huggingface.co/datasets/transferable-samplers/many-peptides-md and model weights https://huggingface.co/transferable-samplers/model-weights." The GitHub codebase LICENSE states: "The core of this repository is licensed under the MIT License. Some files include adaptation of third-party code under other licenses (Apple, Meta, NVIDIA, Klein & Noé). In some cases, these third-party licenses are non-commercial. See NOTICE for details." The dataset is streamed and cached automatically via the accompanying codebase. PDB files are provided as .tar files due to HuggingFace repo limits.
datasetNoise: Both the HuggingFace dataset card and the GitHub README explicitly flag the following data error: "Critical Update: The original 8AA TICA models within subsampled_trajectories/*/8AA/*.npz employed a CA-only atom selection. These models are not valid for comparison to results in our paper." Updated corrected files were uploaded on 15/12/2025. Users who previously downloaded the dataset are instructed to re-download to ensure accurate results. No other noise or quality issues are explicitly described in any of the sources.
datasetSize: The dataset contains MD trajectories for 21,700 peptide sequences total across training, validation, and testing splits. Training split: 21,700 sequences (200 + 1,000 + 1,500 + 2,000 + 3,000 + 4,000 + 10,000 for lengths 2–8 respectively), each simulated for 200 ns with positions and velocities saved every 1 ps. Validation split: 10 sequences each for lengths 2, 4, and 8 (30 total), simulated for 5 µs with data saved every 10 ps. Testing split: 30 sequences each for lengths 2, 4, and 8 (90 total), simulated for 5 µs with data saved every 10 ps. Subsampled sets: 10,000 samples (500 ps downsample of validation/testing trajectories). Webdataset: 4 randomly selected position frames per sequence from 10 ps/frame subsample of training trajectories. HuggingFace dataset viewer is not available as no supported data files were detected automatically.
datasetType: Computational molecular dynamics (MD) trajectory dataset for peptide systems. Contains atomic positions and velocities from MD simulations of 21,700 peptide sequences of lengths 2–8 residues. Data formats provided: (1) Full trajectories (path: trajectories/) in binary format (.bin-based, organized per sequence); (2) PDB files (path: pdb_tarfiles/) provided as .tar archives due to HuggingFace repo limits; (3) Webdataset format (path: webdatasets/single_frames/) as .tar files containing .bin frames with filename format {SEQUENCE}_{TIME}.bin; (4) Subsampled trajectories (path: trajectories_subsampled/) including TICA projection data as .npz files. HuggingFace dataset viewer is not available because no supported data files were detected. The paper describes this as "a novel dataset of molecular dynamics trajectories for peptide systems between 2 and 8 residues."
datasetUpdateMechanism: Both the HuggingFace dataset card and the GitHub README explicitly document the following update: "Updated files (uploaded 15/12/2025) now contain corrected models" replacing invalid 8AA TICA models in subsampled_trajectories/*/8AA/*.npz. The HuggingFace card notes: "Unguarded snapshot_download calls will automatically redownload the relevant files when it detects a change in the repo." The corresponding codebase fix was merged via PR #26 in the GitHub repository.
intendedUse: The HuggingFace dataset card states ManyPeptidesMD was "generated to support research in molecular simulation, machine learning for molecular dynamics, and unnormalized density sampling." The paper explicitly states the dataset and codebase are open-sourced "to further stimulate research into amortized sampling methods and finetuning objectives." The paper describes the broader application context as: protein folding, protein-ligand binding, crystal structure prediction, drug discovery, and material science. The dataset is specifically used to train and evaluate the PROSE model (a 280 million parameter all-atom transferable normalizing flow) and to benchmark it against molecular dynamics and other sampling baselines. The GitHub README states the codebase also supports the work "Scalable Equilibrium Sampling with Sequential Boltzmann Generators" (ICML 2025).
knownBias: The paper explicitly identifies the following limitations in a dedicated "Limitations" section: (1) Transferability assumption: "the transferability of learned samplers including PROSE depends on the assumption that the system is a member of some structured space of energy functions, most commonly the chemical space of molecules. The same applies for the transferability across temperatures." (2) Temperature transfer limitation: "Despite demonstrating surprising abilities to sample from the higher temperature densities, we assume that precise transfer to, more challenging, lower temperatures would require additional conditioning, which PROSE currently lacks." (3) Dataset scope limitation: the dataset covers only peptide sequences of 2–8 residues; the paper notes in the conclusion that "it would be interesting to train PROSE on larger, more diverse, and more realistic datasets such as the recent OMol25 dataset." (4) Dataset error (noted as critical update): the original 8AA TICA models used CA-only atom selection and were not valid, requiring a corrected re-release.

dataCollectionProcess: This dataset is the original Bridge dataset (bridge_orig) from the Open X-Embodiment collection, converted to LeRobot format (huggingface). It is tagged as bridge_orig, openx, and widowx, indicating the data was collected using the WidowX robot arm as part of the Open X-Embodiment collection (huggingface). The Open X-Embodiment collection spans 22 robot types and 527 skills to support cross-platform generalization (arxiv). Existing robot control datasets in this collection "comprise thousands of videos collected in several fixed environments performing limited tasks" (arxiv). The any4lerobot GitHub repository provides the conversion utilities (openx2lerobot module) used to convert the original Open X-Embodiment data into LeRobot format (github).
dataPreprocessing: The dataset was produced by converting the original Open X-Embodiment bridge_orig data into LeRobot format using the openx2lerobot conversion scripts from the any4lerobot repository (github). The any4lerobot toolkit also supports dataset version conversion (LeRobot v1.6 → v2.0 → v2.1 → v3.0), dataset merging, dataset filtering, and dataset sampling as preprocessing utilities (github). The resulting dataset is structured in LeRobot format with the following fields: observation.state (sequence), action (sequence), timestamp (float32), frame_index (int64), episode_index (int64), index (int64), and task_index (int64) (huggingface). The data originates in RLDS format (as indicated by the rlds tag) before conversion to LeRobot format (huggingface).
datasetAvailability: EO-Robotics is released with full openness, including model weights, training code, and all components of the interleaved embodied dataset (arxiv). The dataset is publicly available on HuggingFace at IPEC-COMMUNITY/bridge_orig_lerobot with no access restrictions. License: Apache-2.0 (huggingface).
datasetNoise: Existing robot control datasets (including Open X-Embodiment source data) "comprise thousands of videos collected in several fixed environments performing limited tasks, resulting in high visual similarity between videos" (arxiv).
datasetSize: The exact size of bridge_orig_lerobot is not explicitly stated in any of the sources. The broader Open X-Embodiment collection (of which bridge_orig is one component) contributes to a total aggregated robot control dataset of 1.2 million episodes with 0.13 Trillion tokens, combined across five public sources: AgiBot-World, Open X-Embodiment, RoboMIND, SO100-Community, and IPEC-Franka (arxiv). The HuggingFace dataset viewer was unavailable (JobManagerCrashedError) and no row or episode count is explicitly stated (huggingface).
datasetType: Video dataset for Robotics tasks, in LeRobot format (huggingface). Modality: Video (huggingface). Tags: LeRobot, bridge_orig, rlds, openx, widowx (huggingface). The dataset has one subset (default) and one split (train) (huggingface). Each data record contains the following fields: observation.state (sequence of floats — robot proprioceptive state), action (sequence of floats — robot action), timestamp (float32), frame_index (int64), episode_index (int64), index (int64), task_index (int64) (huggingface). The any4lerobot toolkit also supports conversion back to RLDS format via the lerobot2rlds module (github).
intendedUse: The dataset is used as robot control data for training EO-1, a unified embodied foundation model that integrates multimodal embodied reasoning and real robot control through interleaved vision-text-action pretraining (arxiv). Open X-Embodiment data (of which bridge_orig is a component) is described as providing "critical supervision for policy learning across embodiment generalization and fine-grained manipulation" (arxiv). More broadly, the bridge_orig_lerobot dataset is intended for use with LeRobot-based robot learning projects, enabled by the any4lerobot conversion and preprocessing utilities (github).
knownBias: Existing robot control datasets in this collection (including Open X-Embodiment source data) "comprise thousands of videos collected in several fixed environments performing limited tasks, resulting in high visual similarity between videos" (arxiv).
sensorUsed: The dataset is tagged as "widowx" on HuggingFace, indicating the data was collected using the WidowX robot arm (huggingface). The Open X-Embodiment collection (of which bridge_orig is a component) spans 22 robot types (arxiv).
